questions.question: Define a process. Explain the various states of a process with a state transition diagram. (5 marks)
keyPoints: Process = program in execution with code, data, stack, heap, and PCB
Five states: New, Ready, Running, Waiting, Terminated

Transitions: New->Ready (admitted), Ready->Running (scheduler dispatch), Running->Ready (interrupt/preemption), Running->Waiting (I/O or event wait), Waiting->Ready (I/O or event completion), Running->Terminated (exit)
State transition diagram showing all valid transitions

questions.question: What is a PCB? List and explain its contents. (3 marks)
keyPoints: PCB is a data structure representing each process in the OS
Contains: process state, program counter, CPU registers, CPU scheduling info
Also contains: memory management info, accounting info, I/O status info

questions.question: Explain process creation using fork() and exec() system calls with an example. (5 marks)
keyPoints: fork() creates a child process as a copy of the parent
Returns 0 to child, child's PID to parent
Parent and child execute concurrently after fork()
exec() replaces the process memory with a new program
Typical pattern: fork() then exec() in child to run a new program

questions.question: Compare shared memory and message passing IPC models. (3 marks)
keyPoints: Shared memory: Fast (no system calls for data access), but needs explicit synchronization
Message passing: Easier to implement, OS handles coordination, but slower (system call per message)
Shared memory is preferred for large data, message passing for small amounts and distributed systems
